title: Course rating and slope: why 90 isn't always 90
microFact: Course Rating says how hard a course is for a scratch golfer; Slope says how much harder it gets for the rest of us.
lesson:
Two numbers on every rated tee, and they're why handicaps work across different courses:

COURSE RATING (e.g., 71.8): the score a SCRATCH golfer (0 handicap) is expected to shoot from those tees. Higher than par = tough course.

SLOPE RATING (55 to 155, standard 113): how much MORE difficulty a bogey golfer faces than a scratch golfer. High-slope courses (130+) have forced carries, water, punishing rough — mistakes compound. Low-slope courses are open and forgiving of the exact mishits average golfers produce.

So shooting 95 at a 113-slope course and 95 at a 140-slope monster are very different achievements, and WHS accounts for it: your score differential ≈ (your score − course rating) × 113 ÷ slope.

Practical takeaways: always play RATED tees and note which ones (the app asks); and when picking away courses for confidence-building, glance at the slope — lower slope = friendlier day for a 30-index. Your differentials will be fair either way.

quiz.question: Slope rating measures…
options: How much extra difficulty bogey golfers face vs scratch golfers
quiz.explanation: Slope is the difficulty MULTIPLIER for average players relative to scratch. 113 is standard; 140+ is brutal for high handicaps.
